skill.name: tools-ui
skill.description: Adds React and Next.js guidance for displaying agent tool calls, tool results, progress states, approval requests, and related UI patterns from ui.inference.sh.
skill.summary: Build tool lifecycle interfaces for agent applications.
skill.author: inferen-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backtick, URL, and weak-crypto patterns in SKILL.md. Review found no executable skill code, no prompt injection text, and no credential exfiltration, but the documentation recommends remote npx installation commands and external component registries that users should verify before running.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installation Commands Require User Verification
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends npx commands that fetch components or related skills from remote sources. This is legitimate setup guidance for a UI component skill, but users should inspect the referenced registry content and package behavior before executing the commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly documented as setup instructions and use recognized npx workflows. Risk remains because they retrieve remote content and can change project files when executed.

low_findings.title: External Documentation and Asset Links
low_findings.description: The skill links to hosted documentation, a component documentation page, and an image asset. These URLs support the documented UI package and do not show evidence of data exfiltration or hidden network behavior inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The linked URLs are visible documentation or asset references in markdown. I found no code that sends local data, secrets, or user input to these endpoints.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby or shell backtick execution patterns, but the relevant lines are markdown code fences, inline code, or JSX examples. No Ruby code or backtick-based command execution is present in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content appears inside markdown tables and fenced example blocks. The file is documentation, and I found no executable Ruby or shell script using backticks.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Patterns Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns on lines that contain skill metadata, markdown table text, and a tool description string. No hashing, encryption, signing, or cryptographic API usage appears in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported locations do not contain cryptographic code or algorithm selection. The evidence supports scanner keyword collisions rather than an actual cryptography weakness.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network-Based Package and Registry Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented setup flow uses npx and remote registry URLs. This pattern can alter a user's project and depends on remote content integrity, so users should review sources before running it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and user-triggered, not hidden execution. The risk is operational supply-chain exposure from remote install guidance.

content.value_statement: Agent apps need clear displays for tool calls, progress, approvals, and results. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through practical React and Next.js tool UI patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains UI states for pending, running, approval, success, and error tool calls.
Shows how to display tool call names, arguments, status, and results.
Provides guidance for human approval and denial flows.
Documents icon mapping patterns for common tool names.
Connects tool UI blocks with a larger Agent component pattern.
limitations: It is documentation, not a complete installed component package by itself.
It depends on external ui.inference.sh registry and documentation resources.
It does not implement server-side tool execution or authorization policy.
It assumes a React or Next.js project using compatible component conventions.

use_cases.title: Add Tool Status UI
use_cases.description: Display active agent tool calls with clear status, arguments, and progress indicators.

use_cases.title: Design Approval Workflows
use_cases.description: Create a human approval step before sensitive tool calls execute.

use_cases.title: Review Agent Interfaces
use_cases.description: Assess whether an agent interface communicates tool state and results clearly.

prompt_templates.title: Identify Tool States
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new agent UI
prompt_templates.prompt: List the tool states my agent interface should show and explain what each state means for users.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Tool Card
prompt_templates.scenario: Designing a single tool call display
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a tool call card that shows the tool name, arguments, status, and result summary.

prompt_templates.title: Add Approval Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a plan for adding approve and deny controls to sensitive tool calls in my React app.

prompt_templates.title: Integrate Tool UI With Agent Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced implementation across a full agent interface
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my agent UI flow and recommend how to connect pending, running, approval, success, and error states.

output_examples.input: Show me how tool approval should appear in an agent interface.
output_examples.output: A concise approval panel with the tool name, key arguments, risk context, and approve or deny actions.

output_examples.input: Help me organize tool results in a chat interface.
output_examples.output: A result display plan that separates the tool request, execution status, returned summary, and error state.

output_examples.input: Explain which tool icons to use for common actions.
output_examples.output: A mapping recommendation for search, file, write, delete, send, and fallback tool categories.

best_practices: Show tool status changes immediately so users can understand agent progress.
Require approval for tools that write data, send messages, delete content, or access sensitive resources.
Review remote registry commands before execution and pin trusted versions where possible.
anti_patterns: Do not hide tool arguments when users need to approve an action.
Do not display successful results without preserving error and loading states.
Do not run remote installation commands in production projects without review.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build React and Next.js interfaces for agent tool calls, results, progress, and approvals.

faq.question: Does it execute tools for me?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on UI patterns and component usage for displaying tool lifecycle states.

faq.question: Can it support human approval flows?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents an approval component pattern with approve and deny actions.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium warning because it recommends remote install commands that users should verify.

faq.question: Which projects fit this skill best?
faq.answer: It fits React and Next.js agent applications that need visible tool calls and results.

faq.question: Does it require external resources?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation points to ui.inference.sh resources and npx-based installation commands.
